stem:
A 4-month-old male infant born at term is brought to the regional emergency department in mid-July by his mother. He has had three days of clear coryza and a dry cough, and over the past 24 hours he has been working harder to breathe and is taking only about half of his usual bottle volume. He has had no apnoeas, his nappies are still wet but less so, and he is fully immunised to the current Australian schedule.

He has no significant past medical history and no known sick contacts apart from an older sibling with a runny nose. He is alert and consolable in his mother's arms but visibly tachypnoeic, with audible coarse breath sounds.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 165 bpm, RR 60 /min, SpO2 90% on room air, BP 88/54 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recessions, widespread fine bilateral inspiratory crackles, mild expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.History: Sibling with URTI 5 days ago, no recent travel, no household smoking, term birth at 39 weeks, breast and formula mixed-fed

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, reassess work of breathing at 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in classic bronchiolitis under 12 months and have not shown benefit in trials or in the RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Admit for supportive care with oxygen titrated to SpO2 of at least 92% and nasogastric feeds at two-thirds maintenance
options.rationale: This infant meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 < 92%, feeding under 50% of normal, moderate work of breathing). Management is supportive with oxygen and NG or IV hydration as per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Single dose of oral dexamethasone 3.6 mg and arrange GP follow-up with safety-net advice in 24 hours
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not alter the course of bronchiolitis and are not recommended in any major paediatric guideline, as per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 300 mg empirically for presumed secondary bacterial lower respiratory tract infection in this infant
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated in typical bronchiolitis; bacterial co-infection is uncommon in this age group as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Discharge home on room air with written safety-net advice and arrange GP review within the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Discharge is inappropriate while SpO2 is below 92% and feeding is markedly reduced; admission is required per RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

explanation.summary: Acute bronchiolitis is a viral LRTI of infants under 12 months, most commonly RSV in the Australian winter. Management is supportive: oxygen for SpO2 < 92%, hydration via NG or IV fluids, minimal handling. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics, hypertonic saline, and routine CXR are not recommended.
key_points: RCH admission criteria include SpO2 < 92%, apnoea, feeding < 50% of normal, moderate or severe work of breathing, or social concerns
RSV is the commonest cause; PCR is not routinely required
High-flow nasal cannula oxygen is second-line if standard oxygen fails
Antibiotics, bronchodilators, and steroids have no benefit
explanation.pearls: Apnoea may be the only sign in infants under 6 weeks; have a low threshold to admit.

stem:
A 7-year-old girl with known persistent asthma is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance after 24 hours of escalating wheeze and cough. Her usual preventer is fluticasone 100 microg BD via spacer, with salbutamol as reliever; she has had two previous ED presentations this year and one PICU admission at age 5 requiring high-flow oxygen. At home over the last 6 hours her mother has given hourly salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer with no benefit.

In the ambulance she received back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, plus IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg. There has been minimal symptomatic or objective improvement on arrival. She has had no fevers, no foreign-body history, and no recent oral steroid course in the past 6 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 89% on 8 L/min via nebuliser mask, BP 102/68 mmHg, T 37.2 deg C
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, single-word speech, marked accessory muscle use, silent chest in upper zones, GCS 15 but visibly exhausted
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.28, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.1 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone 100 microg BD, salbutamol PRN, no LABA, no recent oral prednisolone
data_table.History: Previous PICU admission age 5, two ED visits this year, allergic rhinitis, no NSAID exposure

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 220 microg into the lateral thigh, reassess and repeat in 5 minutes if no response
options.rationale: SC adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis-related bronchospasm or settings where inhaled therapy cannot be delivered; it is not first-line escalation in known severe asthma per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 1.1 g (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac and BP monitoring
options.rationale: After back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid have failed in severe acute asthma, IV magnesium sulfate is the next escalation as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation and mechanical ventilation with permissive hypercapnia in the emergency department now
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is high risk for bronchospasm, hypotension and barotrauma; it is reserved for impending respiratory arrest after maximal medical therapy, per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser and observe for stridor reduction over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline targets upper airway oedema in croup, not lower airway bronchospasm; it has no role in this presentation per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Switch from IV hydrocortisone to oral prednisolone 22 mg and continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: She has already received an adequate dose of IV hydrocortisone; switching to oral steroid adds no acute benefit and delays escalation, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Severe / life-threatening acute paediatric asthma escalates as oxygen + back-to-back salbutamol + ipratropium + systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulfate, then IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusion, with early PICU referral as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.
key_points: Severity is assessed by speech, accessory muscle use, mental state, SpO2 and air entry
Silent chest is a sign of life-threatening asthma
IV magnesium dose is 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes
Avoid intubation if at all possible; involve PICU/anaesthetics early
explanation.pearls: Previous PICU admission is a strong predictor of fatal asthma; escalate early and involve PICU before the chest goes truly silent.

stem:
A 2-year-old fully-immunised boy is brought to a metropolitan ED at 2 am by his parents. He had 2 days of clear coryza followed this evening by a sudden barking cough, hoarse voice, and noisy breathing that worsened in the past 6 hours. He is drinking small sips of water and has had no drooling, no neck swelling, and no foreign-body event witnessed.

There is no household tobacco smoke and no recent travel. He weighs 13 kg and has had no previous similar episodes. On the parents' phone video taken 30 minutes ago he had clearly audible inspiratory stridor at rest while sitting calmly.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.9 deg C, HR 140 bpm, RR 36 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 96/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Audible inspiratory stridor at rest, moderate intercostal recession, alert and interactive, mucous membranes moist, no drooling, no neck mass
data_table.History: Fully immunised including Hib and PCV13, no foreign body witnessed, no croup admissions previously

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Single dose of oral dexamethasone 2 mg alone, then discharge home with written croup advice
options.rationale: Discharge is inappropriate while stridor is at rest; only mild croup (no stridor at rest, no recession) can be steroided and discharged per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg plus oral prednisolone 13 mg, reassess work of breathing in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol has no role in upper-airway obstruction from viral laryngotracheitis; dexamethasone is preferred to prednisolone per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 8 mg plus nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000, observe at least 4 hours
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with moderate respiratory distress is moderate croup; RCH and APLS recommend oral dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg, single dose) plus nebulised adrenaline followed by a minimum 4 hour observation period. Adrenaline rebound peaks at 60-90 minutes and resolves by approximately 2 hours; the dexamethasone dose is well absorbed within 30 minutes and exerts mucosal anti-inflammatory effect for 36-48 hours, so a child who remains well at 4 hours post-adrenaline (no stridor at rest, no recession) is covered against rebound by the steroid and can be safely discharged per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Urgent lateral neck radiograph in the ED to exclude epiglottitis before any pharmacological therapy is commenced
options.rationale: Imaging is not indicated in classic croup and risks decompensation; the diagnosis is clinical per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 650 mg stat and call anaesthetics urgently for theatre intubation under inhalational induction
options.rationale: Antibiotics and theatre intubation are reserved for bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis with toxic appearance, drooling or rapid progression, not viral croup per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) is a viral upper airway infection causing inspiratory stridor, barking cough, and respiratory distress in children aged 6 months to 6 years, peaking in the second year. Severity is graded on Westley score or clinically: mild (no stridor at rest, well), moderate (stridor at rest with recession but alert and well perfused), severe (significant respiratory distress, fatigue, agitation, hypoxia), and impending failure (decreased consciousness, cyanosis, exhaustion). Mild croup needs only a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg). Moderate croup adds nebulised adrenaline (5 mL of 1:1000 undiluted) with 4 hours of post-adrenaline observation because of rebound symptoms. Severe croup is managed in a resus bay with continuous monitoring, repeat adrenaline, anaesthetic and ENT involvement, and rarely intubation. The differential of toxic appearance, drooling, tripoding, high fever and stridor without coryza is epiglottitis (rare since Hib vaccination) or bacterial tracheitis - both demand senior airway involvement.
key_points: Severity grading by stridor at rest, recession, perfusion, mental state
Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg is the standard oral dose
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (or 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000, max 5 mL)
Observe at least 4 hours after adrenaline due to rebound risk
explanation.pearls: Why the 4 hour window suffices: nebulised adrenaline alpha-1 vasoconstriction reduces subglottic oedema within 10 minutes; the effect wanes by 90-120 minutes, so any rebound stridor will declare itself well inside a 4 hour observation. By the 4 hour mark, the single dose of oral dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) has been absorbed and is producing sustained anti-inflammatory cover for 36-48 hours, which is why overnight admission is not required for the moderate-croup child who is asymptomatic at 4 hours post-adrenaline (RCH CPG Croup; APLS Australia).

stem:
A previously well 3-year-old girl (weight 15 kg) is brought to her GP with 3 days of fever to 39 deg C, productive cough, and reduced oral intake. Her mother reports decreased activity and one episode of vomiting after a coughing fit this morning. She has not had wheeze, stridor, or paroxysmal whooping spells, and there are no atypical features such as prolonged dry cough or known Mycoplasma exposure at daycare.

She is fully immunised including PCV13. There is no household smoking, no recent travel, and no known TB exposure. She has no drug allergies and has not received antibiotics in the past 3 months. She is tolerating sips of oral fluid in the consultation, is alert and tracking the examiner, with no grunting or head bobbing that would mandate transfer for parenteral therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 deg C, HR 140 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, right-sided focal crackles and bronchial breathing at the right base, no wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, ears and throat unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Office observation only; no bloods or CXR performed at this visit

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 375 mg three times daily for 5 days with safety-net advice and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Uncomplicated CAP in a child > 12 months who is well perfused with SpO2 >= 92% is managed with oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg TDS for 5 days per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory and RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Oral azithromycin 150 mg once daily for 5 days to cover atypical organisms such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae
options.rationale: Macrolides are not first-line in this age group; Streptococcus pneumoniae predominates under 5 years, and Mycoplasma is more relevant from school age, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: Refer immediately to ED for IV benzylpenicillin 900 mg and admission for parenteral therapy and observation
options.rationale: IV therapy is reserved for SpO2 < 92%, dehydration, vomiting all intake, or severe distress; this child can be managed orally per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Arrange urgent outpatient chest x-ray before starting any antibiotic therapy to confirm radiological consolidation today
options.rationale: Routine CXR is not required in uncomplicated CAP managed in the community; it does not change management, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

options.text: Symptomatic management with regular oral paracetamol only, no antibiotics, and review in the clinic in 48 hours
options.rationale: Focal signs with fever in a tachypnoeic child meet criteria for bacterial CAP and warrant antibiotics, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

explanation.summary: Community-acquired pneumonia in well children > 12 months is treated empirically with oral amoxicillin to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae. Macrolides are added where Mycoplasma is likely (older child, atypical features). Admission is required for hypoxia, dehydration, or severe distress.
key_points: Amoxicillin 25 mg/kg TDS for 5 days is the eTG-recommended regimen
Routine CXR is not required for uncomplicated CAP in the community
Admit for SpO2 < 92%, severe distress, inability to tolerate oral intake, or failure of outpatient therapy
Mycoplasma cover (azithromycin) is for school-age and atypical features
explanation.pearls: WHO tachypnoea thresholds are RR > 40 (1-5 yr) and > 50 (2-12 mo); use these alongside focal signs, not in isolation.

stem:
A 24-day-old term male infant is brought to ED at 9 pm with a measured rectal temperature of 38.3 deg C two hours ago and reduced feeding (about 40 percent of usual volumes) for 12 hours. Birth was uncomplicated at 40 weeks, maternal group B Streptococcus status was negative, and his mother received no intrapartum antibiotics. He has been previously well, is exclusively breastfed, and there have been no rashes, vomiting, diarrhoea, cough or coryza.

His 4-year-old sibling has a clear runny nose without fever. He is fully immunised for age (no scheduled vaccinations yet due). He looks pale, is consolable but quiet, and his parents specifically report he is not interacting as he normally does at this time of evening. The ED registrar has performed peripheral blood cultures, urinalysis (catheter), and bedside FBC and CRP, and is now planning antibiotic cover.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.3 deg C rectal, HR 178 bpm, RR 56 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Quiet but rousable, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, no focal signs, soft non-distended abdomen, normal tone
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP, blood culture, urinalysis and urine culture pending; LP not yet performed

lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen, paired with timing of lumbar puncture, is most appropriate?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy now; defer LP until CRP is back to decide whether it is needed
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone does not cover Listeria, which remains a real (though uncommon) pathogen in infants under 4 weeks; deferring LP to await CRP delays diagnosis of meningitis and is contrary to RCH CPG: Febrile child for under-4-week infants.

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin now; defer LP until after antibiotics if the infant is too unwell to safely lie flat
options.rationale: This is the under-7-day neonatal sepsis regimen; for an infant 7 days to under 4 weeks, third-generation cephalosporin cover is added because of higher Gram-negative enteric and pneumococcal risk per RCH CPG.

options.text: Lumbar puncture first, then IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime once CSF is obtained
options.rationale: For a febrile infant aged 7 days to under 4 weeks the RCH-recommended empirical regimen is benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (Listeria, Gram-negatives, and CNS penetration for pneumococcus). LP is performed before antibiotics if the infant is stable; if unstable, antibiotics are given first and LP follows. This 24-day-old is haemodynamically reasonable (CRT 3 s without shock), so LP first is appropriate, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and RCH CPG: Sepsis.

options.text: Await CRP and FBC results before starting antibiotics; perform LP only if CRP is over 40 mg/L
options.rationale: Antibiotic-by-biomarker is unsafe in a febrile under-4-week infant; CRP cut-offs do not rule out invasive bacterial infection at this age, per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin three times daily plus admission for observation; reserve LP for clinical deterioration
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for possible neonatal sepsis or meningitis; IV therapy and LP are mandatory in a febrile infant under 4 weeks, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

explanation.summary: Any temperature >= 38 deg C in an infant < 3 months is treated as possible serious bacterial infection. Infants < 4 weeks have a full septic workup (FBC, CRP, blood culture, urine, LP) and IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime. From 4 weeks to 3 months, ceftriaxone replaces cefotaxime; LP threshold depends on clinical picture.
key_points: Threshold for full septic workup is < 3 months with fever >= 38 deg C
Empirical antibiotics: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime under 4 weeks
LP is part of the workup unless contraindicated
Never delay antibiotics for investigation results
explanation.pearls: Listeria and group B Strep cover (benzylpenicillin) is dropped after about 4-6 weeks unless specific risk factors persist.

stem:
A 4-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community is retrieved to a regional ED after 18 hours of fever, headache, vomiting and increasing drowsiness. She is partially immunised: she received 6-week and 4-month vaccines but missed the 12-month and 18-month boosters, so her Hib and MenC/MenB status is incomplete. Her older brother had a febrile URTI five days ago. The family travelled three weeks ago to visit relatives in an area with known household TB exposure (her uncle is on TB treatment); the child has had no chronic cough or weight loss.

She has a documented maternal history of penicillin-induced urticaria but no history of anaphylaxis. She has a VP shunt placed at age 2 for post-haemorrhagic hydrocephalus, last revised 18 months ago. On the retrieval flight she had two episodes of focal twitching of the right arm lasting 30 seconds each, which resolved without treatment. She has not received any antibiotics or steroids prior to arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 154 bpm, RR 32 /min, BP 88/50 mmHg, SpO2 97% on room air, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6), neck stiffness, photophobia, no rash, soft non-tense fontanelle (closed), VP shunt site clean, no papilloedema on fundoscopy, no focal neurology between events
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 18.6), CRP 248 mg/L, lactate 4.1 mmol/L, Na 128 mmol/L, glucose 6.2 mmol/L, coagulation normal; CT brain pending; LP deferred until imaging
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; no recent antibiotics; no recent steroids
data_table.History: Partial immunisation (missed Hib/MenC/MenB boosters), VP shunt in situ, TB contact 3 weeks ago, maternal penicillin urticaria, sibling URTI 5 days ago

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management before LP?

options.text: IV dexamethasone then IV ceftriaxone plus IV vancomycin, with neurosurgery referral
options.rationale: Empirical therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis under 9 years is IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg; vancomycin is added for possible shunt-related coagulase-negative staph and resistant pneumococcus, and dexamethasone is given just before or with the first antibiotic dose per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Meningitis. Maternal penicillin urticaria does not contraindicate cephalosporins.

options.text: IV aciclovir empirically for HSV encephalitis and defer all empirical antibacterial therapy until LP is performed
options.rationale: HSV encephalitis is a differential given seizures and altered mental state, but the dominant picture (neutrophilia, very high CRP, neck stiffness, photophobia, lactic acidosis) is bacterial; aciclovir alone is inadequate cover per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy without vancomycin or dexamethasone, then proceed directly to LP before any imaging
options.rationale: Vancomycin is required while shunt infection (coagulase-negative staph, Staph aureus) and resistant pneumococcus remain in the differential, and dexamethasone before antibiotics reduces sensorineural deafness in Hib meningitis per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Commence quadruple anti-tuberculous therapy and IV ceftriaxone now, deferring corticosteroid until TB PCR result returns positive
options.rationale: TB meningitis is on the differential but the acute pyogenic picture (CRP 248 mg/L, neutrophilic leucocytosis, 18-hour onset) is not TB; empirical TB therapy is not first-line and steroid timing should not wait per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

options.text: Perform LP immediately before any imaging or antibiotic to ensure maximum microbiological yield from CSF cultures today
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated before imaging when GCS is reduced, focal seizures have occurred, or signs of raised ICP are possible; antibiotics must not be delayed for LP per RCH CPG: Meningitis.

explanation.summary: In bacterial meningitis with reduced GCS, focal seizures, or possible raised ICP, antibiotics and dexamethasone are given first and CT precedes LP. A VP shunt broadens cover to include coagulase-negative staphylococci and Staph aureus, mandating vancomycin alongside ceftriaxone. Maternal urticaria does not contraindicate a third-generation cephalosporin.
key_points: Empirical regimen: ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily plus vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV (shunt risk)
Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg just before or with first antibiotic dose reduces hearing loss in Hib
Defer LP if reduced GCS, focal signs, focal seizures, or coagulopathy
Notify Public Health for invasive meningococcal disease contacts (CDNA)
Indigenous communities have higher rates of invasive Hib and meningococcal disease - vaccine catch-up matters
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemia in meningitis is most often SIADH; fluids are restricted, not bolused, unless shocked.

stem:
A 10-month-old female infant (weight 9.2 kg) presents to her GP with 4 days of intermittent fever to 39 deg C, irritability, and reduced feeding. She has had no cough, coryza, vomiting, or diarrhoea, and no rash. She has no known urinary tract anomalies on the routine antenatal scans. She is fully immunised. A clean-catch urine collected at the GP and sent to the local pathology lab shows leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, and a pure growth of E.

coli > 10^8 CFU/L sensitive to trimethoprim and cephalexin. Renal tract ultrasound has not yet been performed. She is now afebrile after a paracetamol dose and feeding more comfortably; the GP is deciding on follow-up imaging and prophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 deg C, HR 130 bpm, RR 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert and interactive, abdomen soft and non-tender, no flank tenderness elicitable, capillary refill < 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Urine MCS: leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, E. coli > 10^8 CFU/L; FBC and UEC not performed; growth velocity normal on chart
data_table.History: First UTI, fully immunised, no antenatal urinary tract anomaly, breastfed plus solids

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step regarding imaging and follow-up?

options.text: Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) within 6 weeks to assess for vesicoureteric reflux and posterior urethral valves
options.rationale: MCUG is not first-line after a first febrile UTI in an infant; it is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI or abnormal ultrasound per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Arrange a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks and consider further imaging only if abnormal
options.rationale: After a first febrile UTI in an infant under 2 years, renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks is recommended; further imaging (MCUG, DMSA) depends on the ultrasound findings and clinical course per RCH CPG: UTI and NICE guidance.

options.text: No further imaging required as the urinary tract infection has resolved with oral antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is indicated after a first febrile UTI in this age group to detect structural abnormalities and reflux per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Commence long-term prophylactic trimethoprim nocte for 12 months pending nephrology outpatient review
options.rationale: Routine prophylaxis after a first UTI is not recommended; it is considered only after recurrent UTIs or significant reflux per RCH CPG: UTI.

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric urology for outpatient cystoscopy and biopsy within the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: Cystoscopy has no role in the initial workup of a first febrile UTI; imaging is non-invasive and is the standard first step per RCH CPG: UTI.

explanation.summary: First febrile UTI in a child under 2 years is followed by renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks. Atypical UTI (non-E. coli, poor response, septic, palpable bladder) prompts earlier imaging and MCUG/DMSA. Routine prophylaxis is not given.
key_points: Ultrasound is first-line after first febrile UTI under 2 years
MCUG reserved for atypical / recurrent UTI or abnormal ultrasound
DMSA at 4-6 months looks for cortical scarring
Educate on perineal hygiene and prompt fever workup
explanation.pearls: A non-E. coli UTI in an infant is an atypical UTI and triggers earlier and more extensive imaging.

stem:
A 2-year-old boy (weight 12 kg, usual weight 12.5 kg) presents to ED with 2 days of watery non-bloody diarrhoea up to 8 times per day and vomiting around 5 times per day. He has had small sips of water at home but tolerated little. His mother reports he has had only one small wet nappy in the past 12 hours.

He attends daycare where several other children have similar symptoms. He has had no recent travel, no antibiotics, and is fully immunised including rotavirus. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is irritable but consolable in his mother's arms.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 145 bpm, RR 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 95/60 mmHg, capillary refill 2-3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, skin turgor mildly reduced, abdomen soft and non-tender, no peritonism, alert but tired
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.34, HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 3.8 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L, urea 7.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV 0.9% sodium chloride bolus 240 mL over 15 minutes and admit for ongoing rehydration
options.rationale: IV bolus is reserved for shock (CRT > 3 sec, mottling, hypotension); this child has clinical dehydration but is not shocked per RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis.

options.text: Discharge home on ad-lib oral fluids of choice and review by the local GP within 24 hours
options.rationale: Free fluids alone risk hyponatraemia; structured oral or NG rehydration with ORS is required for clinical dehydration per RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis.

options.text: Commence nasogastric oral rehydration solution at 300 mL/hr for 4 hours plus single dose oral ondansetron
options.rationale: Clinical dehydration without shock is treated with rapid NG (or oral) rehydration using ORS at 25 mL/kg/hr for 4 hours, and a single ondansetron dose reduces vomiting and admission rate per RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis.

options.text: Commence empirical oral azithromycin 120 mg daily for presumed bacterial gastroenteritis and continue oral rehydration at home
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated in viral gastroenteritis (the commonest cause) and may prolong carriage in some bacterial pathogens per Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal.

options.text: Commence oral loperamide 1.2 mg to slow stool frequency and discharge home with safety-net advice today
options.rationale: Anti-motility agents are contraindicated in paediatric gastroenteritis due to risk of ileus and toxic megacolon per Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal.

explanation.summary: Clinical dehydration without shock in viral gastroenteritis is managed with rapid enteral rehydration (oral or NG ORS) at approximately 25 mL/kg/hr over 4 hours, with reassessment. A single oral ondansetron dose reduces vomiting and IV escalation. IV fluids are reserved for shock or failed enteral rehydration.
key_points: Assess dehydration clinically; weight change is the most objective sign
Shock: 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus and reassess
Clinical dehydration: rapid NG/oral ORS 25 mL/kg/hr x 4 hours
Avoid loperamide and antibiotics in routine gastroenteritis
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemic dehydration from free-water replacement can present with seizures; aim for ORS and avoid hypotonic IV maintenance fluids.

stem:
A 5-week-old male first-born infant is brought to John Hunter Hospital ED with a 10-day history of vomiting that has become progressively forceful and is now described by his mother as shooting out of his mouth after feeds. He is hungry again immediately after vomiting. The vomitus is milky and non-bilious. Birth weight was 3.4 kg; today he weighs 3.6 kg (his weight at age 4 weeks was 3.7 kg).

He has had fewer wet nappies and his last bowel motion was small and 36 hours ago. He is breastfed. The father reports having had abdominal surgery as an infant for a feeding problem requiring an operation on his stomach outlet. He is alert but slightly sunken in the eyes.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 158 bpm, RR 44 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Mildly sunken anterior fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, visible peristalsis in the epigastrium, palpable olive-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant after a test feed
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.52, HCO3 32 mmol/L, pCO2 50 mmHg, Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.0 mmol/L, Cl 90 mmol/L, glucose 4.0 mmol/L, urea 5.8 mmol/L; abdominal ultrasound pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV 0.9% sodium chloride with potassium at 1.5x maintenance, withhold feeds, refer surgery for pyloromyotomy
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis presents with hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from gastric HCl loss; the airway/surgical priority is fluid and electrolyte correction first, then Ramstedt pyloromyotomy per RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal.

options.text: Proceed directly to Ramstedt pyloromyotomy given clinical certainty, with intra-operative electrolyte correction by the anaesthetic team
options.rationale: Pyloromyotomy is a semi-elective procedure that requires preoperative biochemical correction; induction in an alkalotic, hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic infant carries a high risk of post-operative apnoea regardless of intra-operative fluid management, per RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis.

options.text: Commence oral erythromycin 36 mg four times daily as prokinetic therapy and continue normal volume feeds today
options.rationale: Erythromycin has no role in mechanical gastric outlet obstruction and is associated with pyloric stenosis itself in early infancy per RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis.

options.text: Trial a thickened anti-reflux infant formula for 1 week with PRN paracetamol and no further investigation or imaging
options.rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive and metabolic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not gastro-oesophageal reflux; thickeners delay diagnosis per RCH CPG: GORD.

options.text: Arrange an outpatient upper GI contrast study and gastroenterology review in 1 week with continued oral feeds at home
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the standard imaging and can be done immediately; outpatient contrast delays diagnosis and treatment per RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis.

explanation.summary: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis affects about 1 in 500 Australian infants, with classic 4:1 male predominance and a strong first-born signal. Presentation is at 3-6 weeks with progressive, projectile, non-bilious vomiting in a hungry infant who feeds again immediately. Examination shows visible left-to-right peristalsis and a palpable olive in the right upper quadrant after a test feed. Biochemistry is the hallmark hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria as the kidneys conserve sodium at the expense of hydrogen ions. Ultrasound confirms (pyloric muscle thickness over 3 mm, channel length over 15 mm). Pyloric stenosis is a medical emergency for resuscitation but a surgical semi-elective: fluid and electrolyte correction with 0.9% saline plus added potassium chloride at 1.5x maintenance must precede Ramstedt pyloromyotomy. Operating on an alkalotic infant risks postoperative apnoea.
key_points: Classic biochemistry: hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria
Diagnosis: ultrasound; barium swallow is rarely needed
Correct alkalosis to bicarbonate < 28 mmol/L and chloride > 100 mmol/L before surgery
Definitive treatment: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy
explanation.pearls: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must correct (chloride above 100, bicarbonate under 28, potassium above 3.5) before pyloromyotomy - residual alkalosis suppresses central respiratory drive and risks post-anaesthetic apnoea (RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis).

stem:
An 8-month-old previously well boy (weight 8.5 kg) presents to ED with 12 hours of intermittent episodes of severe distress lasting about 10 minutes, during which he draws his knees to his chest and screams inconsolably, separated by quiet periods of pallor and lethargy. He has vomited four times, the most recent vomit was bile-stained. His mother noted a small amount of redcurrant-jelly-like stool in his nappy 2 hours ago.

He had a viral URTI a week ago. He is fully immunised including rotavirus on schedule. He has no family history of malrotation. He has had no preceding trauma and is on no medications. Between episodes he appears pale but rouses to voice.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 deg C, HR 155 bpm, RR 36 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 92/58 mmHg, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant, no peritonism, no inguinal hernia, normal scrotum, bowel sounds present
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, VBG normal lactate, abdominal ultrasound shows target sign at the hepatic flexure with no free fluid; no signs of perforation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with regular oral anti-emetics and review in 24 hours if symptoms persist or worsen overnight
options.rationale: Confirmed ileocolic intussusception requires urgent reduction; discharge risks bowel ischaemia and perforation per RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Refer immediately to interventional radiology for air enema reduction after fluid resuscitation and paediatric surgical review
options.rationale: Confirmed ileocolic intussusception without peritonism or perforation is reduced by image-guided air (or contrast) enema in a centre with paediatric surgical backup, after fluid resuscitation per RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy without attempting non-operative air or contrast enema reduction first in radiology
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonism, or perforation; non-operative reduction is first-line in stable patients per RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Commence empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and observe on the paediatric ward for 24 hours pending repeat examination
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone do not address the mechanical obstruction; definitive reduction is needed promptly per RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Arrange outpatient barium meal contrast study to confirm the diagnosis before any further action or referral today
options.rationale: Ultrasound has already confirmed the diagnosis; further outpatient imaging delays reduction and increases ischaemic risk per RCH CPG: Intussusception.

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception in infants 3-36 months presents with episodic colic, vomiting (later bilious), pallor, and redcurrant-jelly stools. Diagnosis is by ultrasound (target sign). Treatment in stable patients is image-guided air enema reduction in a paediatric surgical centre. Peritonism, perforation, or failed enema mandates surgery.
key_points: Triad (colic + vomiting + redcurrant stool) is present in less than half of cases
Ultrasound target / doughnut sign is diagnostic
Air enema reduction success rate around 75-90%
Recurrence rate around 10%, usually within 24 hours; observe accordingly
explanation.pearls: A pathological lead point (Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma, HSP-related haematoma) is more likely in children > 2 years.

stem:
A 28-week gestation preterm female infant, now day 12 of life and corrected age 29+5, is being cared for in a tertiary NICU. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and reached full enteral feeds of expressed breast milk at 150 mL/kg/day on day 9. Over the past 6 hours she has had increasing apnoeas requiring stimulation, two episodes of bilious aspirate from the orogastric tube totalling 6 mL, and a distended tense abdomen with visible bowel loops.

Her last stool was loose and faintly blood-streaked. She is on caffeine citrate, vitamins, and iron; she has had no recent transfusion and no umbilical lines remain. Her mother had clear liquor and received a full antenatal steroid course; there was no chorioamnionitis. On examination she is irritable and mottled.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.2 deg C axillary, HR 178 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 88% on CPAP FiO2 0.35, BP 48/28 mmHg, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Distended tense abdomen with visible loops, reduced bowel sounds, palpable tender mass-effect in right lower quadrant, no inguinal hernia, perfusion poor
data_table.Investigations: WCC 3.1 x10^9/L (neutrophils 0.8), platelets 78 x10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.0 mmol/L, abdominal x-ray shows pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas, no free intraperitoneal air
data_table.Medications: Caffeine citrate, vitamins, iron supplement; full enteral feeds since day 9; no current antibiotics
data_table.History: 28-week preterm, antenatal steroids complete, no chorioamnionitis, no umbilical line since day 7

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cease feeds, free-draining orogastric tube, IV ampicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, 10 mL/kg saline bolus, refer paediatric surgery
options.rationale: Bell stage II NEC with systemic compromise requires gut rest with OG decompression, triple antibiotic cover (ampicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole), cautious fluid resuscitation, and surgical involvement; pneumoperitoneum or clinical deterioration triggers laparotomy per RCH NICU: NEC and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at half rate, observe abdominal girth hourly, send blood cultures and repeat AXR in 12 hours
options.rationale: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas is established NEC; continued feeds risk progression to perforation per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Proceed straight to laparotomy in NICU without antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, or further imaging while orogastric tube remains clamped
options.rationale: Without pneumoperitoneum or peritonitis the initial approach is medical with surgical standby; resuscitation and antibiotics precede any operation per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Continue current feed volume, add probiotic supplementation, send blood cultures, and recheck abdominal x-ray in 12 hours
options.rationale: Probiotics are a primary-prevention strategy in selected preterm infants and have no role in active NEC management per RCH NICU: NEC.

options.text: Convert to donor expressed breast milk, continue current ventilatory support and feed volume, with no antibiotics indicated
options.rationale: Donor milk does not treat established NEC and antibiotics are required given systemic signs and biochemical derangement per RCH NICU: NEC.

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is staged by Bell criteria. Stage II (radiological pneumatosis with systemic signs) is managed with bowel rest, OG decompression, parenteral nutrition, triple antibiotic cover, and surgical involvement. Stage III (perforation, shock) requires laparotomy.
key_points: Risk factors: prematurity, formula vs breast milk, hypoxic-ischaemic events, sepsis
Pneumatosis intestinalis on AXR is pathognomonic
Triple cover: ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole (or piperacillin-tazobactam)
NPO 7-14 days depending on stage; TPN required
Indication for laparotomy: pneumoperitoneum, deterioration despite medical therapy
explanation.pearls: Thrombocytopenia and falling platelet trend correlate with NEC severity; track them serially.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl (weight 28 kg) with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to ED with 2 days of vomiting, abdominal pain, polyuria, and polydipsia, and increasing drowsiness over the past 4 hours. She has had no diarrhoea, no head injury, and no preceding focal neurology. She has not received any insulin yet. Her parents report a recent viral URTI two weeks ago and weight loss of 3 kg over 6 weeks.

On arrival she is moderately dehydrated with deep sighing respirations. Her capillary blood glucose is 28 mmol/L and capillary ketones are 5.6 mmol/L. She has had no IV fluids or antibiotics yet.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 132 bpm, RR 34 /min Kussmaul, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 96/62 mmHg, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Examination: Kussmaul respiration, acetone breath, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, soft abdomen with mild generalised tenderness, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.08, HCO3 8 mmol/L, pCO2 18 mmHg, anion gap 24, Na 132 mmol/L (corrected 136), K 5.4 mmol/L, glucose 28 mmol/L, ketones 5.6 mmol/L, urea 9.2 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L
data_table.Medications: Nil; new diagnosis of T1DM, no insulin started
data_table.History: Recent viral URTI, weight loss 3 kg over 6 weeks, family history of coeliac disease

lead_in: After airway and breathing assessment, what is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: IV insulin bolus 0.1 unit/kg now then fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/hr without prior fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: An insulin bolus is not given in paediatric DKA; insulin is started as a fixed-rate infusion 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation begins, at 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline and RCH CPG: DKA.

options.text: 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus, then deficit replacement over 48 hours with insulin at 0.05 unit/kg/hr later
options.rationale: Paediatric DKA fluid resuscitation is cautious (10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 30-60 min unless shocked), with deficit replaced over 48 hours and insulin started 1-2 hours after fluid commencement at 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr to minimise cerebral oedema risk per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline and RCH CPG: DKA.

options.text: Give 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and repeat as needed, then commence insulin concurrently with the second bolus
options.rationale: Rapid large fluid boluses (>10 mL/kg) increase cerebral oedema risk in paediatric DKA and are reserved for true shock; insulin is not started immediately per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 1 hour to correct metabolic acidosis before commencing fluids or insulin therapy
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is associated with worse outcomes including cerebral oedema and hypokalaemia, and is not recommended in paediatric DKA except for life-threatening hyperkalaemia per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

options.text: Give an immediate dose of IV mannitol 0.5 g/kg as prophylaxis against cerebral oedema, then commence fluids and insulin
options.rationale: Mannitol is reserved for treatment of overt cerebral oedema (e.g. headache, GCS deterioration, hypertension, bradycardia), not prophylaxis per APEG/ISPAD DKA Guideline.

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management prioritises cautious fluid replacement and delayed insulin to minimise cerebral oedema. Fluid resuscitation: only 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 30-60 min unless shocked; deficit (5-7%) replaced evenly over 48 hours. Insulin 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/hr starts 1-2 hours after fluid begins. Bicarbonate is avoided.
key_points: Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of mortality in paediatric DKA
Warning signs: headache, GCS drop, bradycardia, hypertension, focal neurology
Insulin delayed 1-2 hours; no bolus
Add 10% dextrose to fluids when glucose < 14-17 mmol/L
Monitor potassium hourly; replace early
explanation.pearls: Corrected sodium that fails to rise (or falls) as glucose falls predicts cerebral oedema.

stem:
A 5-year-old boy (weight 19 kg) with known refractory focal epilepsy on levetiracetam and sodium valproate is brought into the resuscitation bay by ambulance with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at home 35 minutes ago. Paramedics gave intranasal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg en route at 15 minutes and again at 25 minutes with no termination. On arrival he is still actively convulsing.

His mother reports a recent vomiting illness over 2 days with reduced oral intake, missed afternoon levetiracetam yesterday, and no head injury. There is no fever history and no household toxin exposure. He has IV access in the right antecubital fossa established by paramedics. Glucose by bedside meter is 4.2 mmol/L. A 12-lead ECG performed in the bay shows narrow complex sinus tachycardia with QTc 410 ms. Resuscitation team is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 deg C tympanic, HR 165 bpm, RR 30 /min self-ventilating, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min non-rebreather, BP 110/72 mmHg, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity, eyes deviated right, gag intact, no rash, fontanelle closed, no focal injury
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.2 mmol/L, VBG pH 7.22 lactate 5.4 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, calcium normal, paracetamol/salicylate negative, levetiracetam level pending
data_table.Medications: Levetiracetam 30 mg/kg/day, sodium valproate 20 mg/kg/day; two doses intranasal midazolam already given in ambulance
data_table.History: Refractory focal epilepsy, recent gastroenteritis, missed dose 24 hours ago, no allergies

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Give a third dose of IV midazolam 3 mg now and repeat at 5 minutes if seizure activity persists
options.rationale: After two failed benzodiazepine doses the seizure is benzodiazepine-resistant; further doses increase respiratory depression without improving termination per APLS Paediatric advanced life support and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Load with IV levetiracetam 760 mg over 5 minutes with cardiac monitoring, then prepare RSI with thiopentone
options.rationale: After two benzodiazepine doses, second-line therapy in paediatric convulsive status is IV levetiracetam (40-60 mg/kg) or phenytoin/fosphenytoin; thiopentone (or propofol) RSI is third-line if seizure persists, per APLS algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Load with IV sodium valproate 760 mg given that he is already on this medication for his epilepsy
options.rationale: Loading with the existing maintenance agent risks toxicity (especially valproate in a child with possible viral illness and hepatic stress) and is not first-choice second-line per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate 950 mg over 20 minutes as second-line anticonvulsant therapy for refractory status
options.rationale: IV magnesium is for eclamptic seizures and hypomagnesaemic seizures, not idiopathic refractory status epilepticus in a euglycaemic normokalaemic normocalcaemic child per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

options.text: Give IV 10% dextrose 38 mL empirically for possible hypoglycaemia and reassess seizure activity in 5 minutes
options.rationale: Bedside glucose is 4.2 mmol/L; the seizure is not hypoglycaemic and additional dextrose does not address ongoing seizure activity per APLS Paediatric advanced life support.

explanation.summary: Paediatric convulsive status epilepticus that has failed two benzodiazepine doses (about 25-30 min) escalates to a second-line agent: levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg or phenytoin/fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg, monitoring rhythm. Refractory status (45+ minutes) is managed with anaesthetic agents (thiopentone, propofol, midazolam infusion) and intubation in PICU.
key_points: Limit benzodiazepines to two doses to avoid cumulative respiratory depression
Second-line: levetiracetam (preferred for cardiac stability) or fosphenytoin/phenytoin
Check glucose, electrolytes, toxicology, and look for reversible cause
Third-line: thiopentone or propofol with RSI
Continuous EEG once intubated to assess electrographic seizures
explanation.pearls: EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials showed levetiracetam and phenytoin equivalent for second-line termination; levetiracetam is often preferred for safety.

stem:
A previously well 18-month-old boy is brought to ED after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 2 minutes. He had been febrile since the morning with clear coryza and a tympanic temperature of 39.0 deg C. After the seizure he was drowsy for 15 minutes and then returned to baseline, drinking and interacting normally with his parents. There is a paternal history of childhood febrile seizures and he is fully immunised.

On arrival he is alert and interactive with a temperature of 38.4 deg C, no neck stiffness, no rash, and no focal neurology. The ear examination shows a right bulging erythematous tympanic membrane. He has not had recent antibiotics and there is no head injury.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 deg C, HR 140 bpm, RR 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert and interactive, fontanelle closed, no neck stiffness, no rash, ear examination shows a right bulging red tympanic membrane, no focal neurology
data_table.History: Paternal history of febrile seizures, fully immunised, single seizure under 15 min, returned to baseline

lead_in: Which combination of investigation and management is most appropriate?

options.text: Non-contrast CT brain and admission for 24-hour observation; commence regular paracetamol
options.rationale: Routine neuroimaging is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology and an identified febrile focus, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone because of the seizure during fever
options.rationale: LP is not indicated in a fully immunised over-12-month-old with a simple febrile seizure who has returned to baseline without meningism, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure. LP-before-treat reasoning is the anti-pattern tested here.

options.text: Prophylactic oral sodium valproate to prevent recurrence with neurology outpatient referral
options.rationale: Long-term anticonvulsants are not used after simple febrile seizures, even with positive family history, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

options.text: No investigations; treat the acute otitis media per eTG criteria, provide written first-aid advice, discharge home
options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure (single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, 6 months to 6 years) with an identified bacterial focus (right bulging TM in a child under 2 with severe symptoms meets eTG criteria for amoxicillin 30 mg/kg q8h for 5 days). No investigations are required. Reassurance and written first-aid advice are standard per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Admit overnight for continuous EEG and reassess for discharge in the morning
options.rationale: EEG is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with full recovery, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizure is a single generalised seizure under 15 minutes in a child 6 months to 6 years with fever and no CNS infection. Management is to identify and treat the febrile focus, reassure the family, and provide written first-aid advice. Investigations are guided by the febrile focus, not the seizure.
key_points: Simple: single, generalised, < 15 min, full recovery
Complex: focal, > 15 min, or recurrent within 24 hours
Recurrence around 30%; lifetime epilepsy risk slightly higher than baseline but still low
Antipyretics do not prevent recurrence
Family education and safety advice is the cornerstone
explanation.pearls: A child who has not returned to baseline mental status within an hour deserves a more careful look for meningitis or encephalitis.

stem:
A 3-year-old previously well boy presents on day 6 of fever. His mother reports daily fevers peaking at 39.5 deg C, unresponsive to alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, cracked red lips, a strawberry tongue, a polymorphous truncal rash, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy with one node 1.8 cm. His hands and feet are red and swollen. He has no respiratory symptoms, no diarrhoea, and no household contacts with rash.

He is fully immunised. Initial bloods show CRP 138 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, platelets 530 x10^9/L, ALT 78 U/L. Urine microscopy shows sterile pyuria. Echocardiography is being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 32 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 96/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection (non-exudative), cracked lips, strawberry tongue, polymorphous truncal rash, red swollen palms and soles, one cervical node 1.8 cm
data_table.Investigations: Echocardiogram pending; CRP 138 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, platelets 530 x10^9/L

lead_in: Which combination of immediate therapy and aspirin dosing is most appropriate?

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin until afebrile for 48 hours, then switch to low-dose antiplatelet aspirin until inflammatory markers and echocardiogram are normal
options.rationale: He meets clinical criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 principal features) and remains within the day-10 window where IVIG most reduces coronary aneurysm risk. Australian practice (RCH CPG) uses IVIG 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin acutely, stepping down to low-dose antiplatelet aspirin once afebrile and continuing until coronary status is confirmed.

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus oral prednisolone as primary adjunct; withhold aspirin because of risk of Reye syndrome in a child under 5
options.rationale: Aspirin is standard in Kawasaki disease and the Reye-syndrome concern does not override its use in this indication. Adjunctive steroids are reserved for high-risk patients (Kobayashi or Egami score, age under 12 months, persistent fever) or IVIG-resistant disease, not first-line in this child, per RCH CPG.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin three times daily for 10 days for presumed group A streptococcal scarlet fever
options.rationale: Scarlet fever does not cause non-purulent conjunctivitis or extremity desquamation; missing Kawasaki disease delays IVIG and increases aneurysm risk per RCH CPG.

options.text: Defer IVIG until echocardiography is completed and confirms coronary changes, then treat
options.rationale: IVIG should be given on clinical grounds within 10 days of fever onset; echocardiography is for baseline assessment of coronaries, not a gate to treatment, per RCH CPG and AHA 2017.

options.text: Reassurance and antipyretics; arrange paediatric outpatient review if fever persists beyond day 10
options.rationale: Delaying treatment beyond day 10 substantially increases coronary aneurysm risk; treatment should be commenced now, per RCH CPG.

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a clinical diagnosis: fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 of bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, mucositis, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy > 1.5 cm. Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg and aspirin, ideally within 10 days, with echocardiography to assess coronary arteries.
key_points: Treat within 10 days of fever onset to minimise coronary aneurysm risk
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion
Aspirin (anti-inflammatory dose during fever, then low-dose) per local protocol
Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks, 6-8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Incomplete Kawasaki disease (fewer features) is more common in infants under 6 months and still requires echo and treatment if criteria with inflammatory markers and echo features support it.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to his GP with a 5-day history of a non-blanching palpable purpuric rash over the buttocks and extensor surfaces of both lower legs, accompanied by colicky abdominal pain and swelling of both ankles. He had a viral URTI 2 weeks ago that resolved spontaneously. His parents have noted no haematuria but the urine has been slightly darker for 2 days.

He is otherwise well and is feeding and playing. He is fully immunised. There is no family history of bleeding disorders or renal disease. His weight and growth are on the 50th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0 deg C, HR 105 bpm, RR 22 /min, SpO2 99%, BP 112/74 mmHg (above 95th centile for age/height)
data_table.Examination: Palpable non-blanching purpura on buttocks and lower legs, bilateral ankle swelling, mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without peritonism, no scrotal swelling, no joint redness
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: blood 2+, protein 2+, no leucocytes; FBC platelets 320 x10^9/L; UEC normal; coagulation normal; urine PCR pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol PRN only, no urinalysis or blood pressure monitoring, and review in 2 weeks if rash persists
options.rationale: HSP requires structured follow-up of blood pressure and urinalysis to detect nephritis; passive review is inadequate per RCH CPG: HSP.

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone for 4 weeks as routine first-line therapy regardless of severity or renal involvement
options.rationale: Routine corticosteroids do not prevent or change the natural history of HSP nephritis; they are reserved for severe abdominal pain, orchitis, severe arthritis, or proven nephritis per RCH CPG: HSP.

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric surgery for laparotomy to exclude ileoileal intussusception before further outpatient review or investigation
options.rationale: Ileoileal intussusception is an HSP complication but requires ultrasound only if vomiting, severe pain, or PR bleeding are present; routine surgical referral is not first-line per RCH CPG: HSP.

options.text: Paracetamol/NSAID for pain, weekly urinalysis and BP for 6 months, prompt review for new haematuria or proteinuria
options.rationale: HSP management is supportive, with structured BP and urinalysis monitoring for 6-12 months to detect nephritis; current BP and urine findings are already at threshold for closer review per RCH CPG: HSP.

options.text: Commence aspirin as an anti-platelet agent and review urinalysis and blood pressure monthly for 6 months
options.rationale: Aspirin has no role in HSP and risks bleeding complications; supportive care is the recommended approach per RCH CPG: HSP.

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is a small-vessel vasculitis with palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain, and renal involvement. Most cases are self-limiting. Long-term morbidity is from nephritis; BP and urinalysis are monitored for 6-12 months. Steroids are reserved for specific severe features.
key_points: Tetrad: palpable purpura (mandatory), arthritis, abdominal pain, renal involvement
Nephritis can develop weeks after presentation; ongoing urinalysis is essential
Steroids: severe abdominal pain, orchitis, severe arthritis, proven nephritis
Refer to nephrology for persistent proteinuria, hypertension, or impaired function
explanation.pearls: An HSP child with bilious vomiting and severe pain may have ileoileal intussusception (not the classic ileocolic) and needs urgent imaging.

stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote community presents to a regional ED with a 3-week history of intermittent migratory polyarthritis affecting his right knee, then his left ankle, and now his right wrist. His mother reports an episode of sore throat 5 weeks ago that resolved without medical review. Over the past week he has been more breathless on exertion and his teacher noted involuntary jerking movements of his hands during class. He has no significant past medical history and is fully immunised. There is a family history of rheumatic heart disease in his maternal grandmother.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, HR 118 bpm, RR 24 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 110/68 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild pallor, choreiform movements of the hands and tongue, soft pansystolic murmur loudest at the apex radiating to the axilla, mild left ankle swelling, no rash, no subcutaneous nodules, lungs clear
data_table.Investigations: ESR 88 mm/hr, CRP 64 mg/L, ASOT 1200 IU/mL, anti-DNase B 1800 U/mL, ECG PR interval 220 ms, echocardiogram shows mild mitral regurgitation with normal LV function, throat swab pending
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; no recent antibiotic course
data_table.History: Sore throat 5 weeks ago, family history of RHD, lives in RHD-endemic community

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate single-best diagnostic and management decision?

options.text: Diagnose definite acute rheumatic fever, commence IM benzathine penicillin, aspirin, secondary prophylaxis register, cardiology referral
options.rationale: He fulfils high-risk Jones criteria for definite ARF (2 major: chorea and carditis with mitral regurgitation; supportive evidence of preceding GAS via ASOT/anti-DNase B). Treatment is benzathine penicillin, anti-inflammatory therapy, and lifelong secondary prophylaxis enrolment per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose juvenile idiopathic arthritis and commence weekly oral methotrexate 10 mg/m^2 with folic acid and rheumatology follow-up
options.rationale: Migratory polyarthritis with carditis, chorea and high streptococcal serology is ARF, not JIA; JIA does not cause chorea or mitral regurgitation per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose viral myocarditis and admit for supportive care with telemetry, paracetamol for fever, no antibiotics or anti-inflammatory therapy
options.rationale: The constellation of migratory arthritis, chorea, carditis and elevated streptococcal titres is ARF; penicillin is required for residual GAS and secondary prophylaxis per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Diagnose infective endocarditis and start IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin empirically pending three sets of blood cultures
options.rationale: There are no peripheral stigmata, no fever pattern of endocarditis, and the picture (chorea, migratory arthritis, ASOT rise) is ARF; blood cultures should be sent but empirical endocarditis therapy is not indicated per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Diagnose probable functional movement disorder, reassure the family, and refer to community psychology for outpatient assessment review
options.rationale: Sydenham chorea is organic and pathognomonic for ARF in high-risk populations; functional diagnosis is inappropriate per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

explanation.summary: In high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, Maori and Pacific peoples), the Jones criteria are modified: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor manifestations of GAS infection establish a definite ARF diagnosis. Treatment includes benzathine penicillin G IM, anti-inflammatory therapy for arthritis/carditis (aspirin or naproxen), and lifelong-or-prolonged secondary prophylaxis enrolment.
key_points: High-risk Jones major: carditis, polyarthritis or aseptic monoarthritis or polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Evidence of preceding GAS: ASOT or anti-DNase B rise, positive throat swab/culture, recent scarlet fever
Benzathine penicillin G dose: 1.2 million units IM (> 20 kg) every 3-4 weeks
Echocardiography is now mandatory in suspected ARF
Notify the state RHD register
explanation.pearls: Chorea may appear weeks after the initial illness and can be isolated; do not exclude ARF for absence of arthritis.

stem:
A 3-day-old breastfed term male infant (39+5 weeks gestation, birth weight 3.3 kg, current weight 3.1 kg, weight loss 6 percent) is reviewed in the midwife-led postnatal clinic. His mother is blood group O Rh positive; baby blood group is A Rh positive. He has appeared progressively yellow since 24 hours of life. He is feeding around 8 times in 24 hours but the mother is finding latch difficult.

He is alert when awake, has had wet nappies, and one transitional stool. There is no family history of G6PD deficiency or hereditary spherocytosis and no household exposure to mothballs. He has had no fever, no lethargy, and no high-pitched cry. Transcutaneous bilirubin in clinic is 290 micromol/L; a serum total bilirubin is taken.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C axillary, HR 140 bpm, RR 42 /min, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, jaundice extending to abdomen and thighs, no scleral icterus initially noted, normal tone, no hepatosplenomegaly, no petechiae, anterior fontanelle soft
data_table.Investigations: Serum total bilirubin 305 micromol/L (unconjugated 295, conjugated 10), Hb 165 g/L, reticulocytes mildly raised, DAT positive (anti-A), G6PD pending
data_table.History: Term, breastfed, ABO setup (mother O, baby A), 6% weight loss, latch difficulty

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the family that this is benign physiological jaundice not requiring intervention and review by midwife in 48 hours
options.rationale: A DAT-positive ABO setup with bilirubin near the phototherapy line is not physiological; observation alone risks progression to kernicterus per RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Plot bilirubin on age-in-hours nomogram and commence intensive phototherapy if at or above treatment line
options.rationale: Bilirubin is interpreted against the age-in-hours nomogram with adjustment for haemolysis risk factors (DAT positive ABO incompatibility); phototherapy is started at the corresponding line per RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy and AAP 2022 Hyperbilirubinemia Guideline.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Stop breastfeeding immediately for 48 hours, switch to exclusive infant formula feeds, and recheck serum bilirubin level tomorrow
options.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended; phototherapy and feeding support are the correct approach per RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Arrange exchange transfusion now in the neonatal unit irrespective of nomogram position or response to commenced phototherapy
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin levels approaching the exchange line, signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, or failure of intensive phototherapy per AAP 2022 Hyperbilirubinemia Guideline.

options.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone to induce hepatic glucuronyl transferase conjugation and review bilirubin in 48 hours
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is not part of acute neonatal jaundice management; it is occasionally used in Crigler-Najjar but not here per RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice is plotted on an age-in-hours nomogram. Phototherapy thresholds are lowered when haemolysis is present (DAT positive, G6PD deficiency). Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin at the exchange line or signs of bilirubin encephalopathy. Feeding optimisation reduces enterohepatic recirculation.
key_points: Always interpret bilirubin against age-in-hours and risk factors
Conjugated bilirubin > 25 micromol/L or > 20% of total is pathological and warrants workup
DAT-positive ABO/Rh setup are haemolytic risk factors
Acute bilirubin encephalopathy: lethargy, high-pitched cry, hypertonia, retrocollis
explanation.pearls: Jaundice in the first 24 hours of life is always pathological and warrants urgent investigation.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP for her routine developmental check by her parents, who are concerned about her behaviour. She was born at term with no antenatal or perinatal concerns and is fully immunised. She walks well, climbs, scribbles, and feeds herself with a spoon. However, she has not yet spoken any single words and does not respond consistently to her name, although she will turn to loud environmental sounds.

She does not point to indicate interest, prefers to play alone with lining up objects, and rarely makes sustained eye contact. There is no family history of hearing loss, speech delay, or autism, and she has no significant medical history. Her parents speak English at home only.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 110 bpm, RR 26 /min
data_table.Examination: Growth on 50th centile for weight, height, and head circumference, no dysmorphic features, normal cardiovascular and neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: Newborn hearing screen passed at birth, no formal audiology since
data_table.History: Term birth, monolingual English household, no family history of ASD or hearing loss

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the parents that single-word speech can be delayed to 24 months in girls and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Absence of single words plus loss/limited joint attention, name-response, and pointing at 18 months are red flags requiring further evaluation now, not delayed review per RCH CPG: Developmental milestones.

options.text: Refer for formal audiology assessment and to community paediatrics or child development service for ASD evaluation
options.rationale: A child with absent words, limited joint attention, name-response and social interest at 18 months requires (1) formal audiology to exclude hearing loss and (2) paediatric/child development service review for ASD assessment per RCH CPG: Developmental milestones and RACGP guidance.

options.text: Request an urgent MRI brain in the next 2 weeks to exclude a structural intracranial cause of speech delay
options.rationale: MRI is not first-line in this presentation without focal neurology, macro/microcephaly, or seizures per RCH CPG: Developmental milestones.

options.text: Initiate chromosomal microarray testing and Fragile X DNA analysis in the GP rooms today before further specialist referral
options.rationale: Genetic testing has a role in confirmed global developmental delay or ASD but is sequenced after audiology and developmental assessment per RACGP: Child development assessment.

options.text: Refer for community speech therapy alone and continue developmental surveillance through Maternal and Child Health nurse review
options.rationale: Speech therapy alone misses the broader autism-spectrum picture and the need to exclude hearing loss first per RCH CPG: Developmental milestones.

explanation.summary: Red flags at 18 months include no single words, no pointing, no response to name, limited joint attention and lack of sustained eye contact. Workup begins with formal audiology (newborn screen does not exclude later or acquired hearing loss) and a paediatric/child development service referral for autism assessment alongside developmental support.
key_points: 18-month red flags: no single words, no point, no name response, no shared enjoyment
Always exclude hearing loss with formal audiology
Refer to community paediatrics, child development service, or developmental paediatrician
Speech therapy, OT, and ASD-specific intervention can begin in parallel
explanation.pearls: Bilingual exposure does not delay overall language acquisition; do not attribute delay to bilingualism.

stem:
A 5-month-old boy is brought to ED by his 18-year-old mother with a 3-day history of irritability and reduced feeding. The mother attends with her current partner of 3 weeks. She gives an inconsistent history: she initially says the baby has not fallen, then later that he rolled from a couch yesterday. Birth was at term with no concerns; he is not yet rolling independently.

He attends the maternal and child health nurse irregularly and has missed his 4-month immunisations. The partner is unemployed and they have moved house three times in the past 6 months. There is no formal record of family violence, but the mother smells of cigarette smoke and appears withdrawn. On examination there is a 1 cm bruise on the left cheek, a torn upper labial frenulum, and several small bruises on the anterior shins.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 150 bpm, RR 38 /min, SpO2 100% on room air, BP 88/52 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Alert and irritable; left cheek bruise 1 cm, torn upper frenulum with small clot, small bruises over anterior shins, anterior fontanelle soft, no retinal haemorrhages on fundoscopy by senior, normal tone, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, coagulation normal, UEC normal, LFTs mildly raised ALT 95 U/L, skeletal survey pending, head imaging pending
data_table.History: Missed 4-month immunisations, irregular MCH visits, three house moves in 6 months, new partner 3 weeks, inconsistent injury history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, head imaging (CT then MRI), ophthalmology review, mandatory child protection notification
options.rationale: Bruising and a torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant are sentinel injuries; an inconsistent history and social risk factors compound concern. Workup includes skeletal survey, neuroimaging, and ophthalmology, with mandatory reporting per RCH CPG: Child abuse and the relevant state child protection legislation.

options.text: Discharge home with community social work follow-up arranged in the next 2 weeks and GP review tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Discharging a non-mobile infant with multiple sentinel injuries and an inconsistent history risks further harm; admission for full workup and immediate reporting is mandatory per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Order FBC and coagulation studies only, and if normal, attribute the bruising to a presumed undiagnosed inherited bleeding disorder
options.rationale: Normal coagulation does not exclude non-accidental injury; further imaging and child protection notification are required per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Explain to the mother that you suspect non-accidental injury and ask the partner to leave the room before further history-taking
options.rationale: Direct disclosure of suspicion in this manner can escalate risk to the child and provoke removal from care; suspicion is communicated through child protection processes, not unilateral confrontation per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

options.text: Arrange routine outpatient paediatrician review and community social worker phone call within the next 7 days for assessment
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral is unsafe for an infant with sentinel injuries; immediate inpatient workup and notification are required per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

explanation.summary: A bruise in a non-mobile infant, a torn frenulum, and any inconsistent history are sentinel injuries for child physical abuse. Required workup includes skeletal survey, neuroimaging (CT then MRI if positive or symptoms suggest), retinal examination, coagulation screen, LFTs, and mandatory child protection notification. The medical practitioner is a mandatory reporter in every Australian state and territory.
key_points: If a baby isn't cruising, they don't have bruising
Sentinel injuries: bruise in non-mobile child, torn frenulum, intraoral injury, ear injury, genital injury
Mandatory reporting is by suspicion, not proof
Document injuries with photographs and clear anatomical drawings
Engage social work and paediatric forensic services early
explanation.pearls: Document the history in the carer's words verbatim, with quotation marks; later inconsistencies are critical evidence.

stem:
A 4-year-old girl is brought to the GP with a 3-week history of progressive pallor, lethargy, intermittent low-grade fever, and bone pain that wakes her at night, particularly in both legs. Over the past week her parents have noticed easy bruising on her shins and trunk, a few nose bleeds, and that she has stopped wanting to walk to kindergarten.

She has had no preceding viral illness within the last 6 weeks and has not been on antibiotics or NSAIDs. She is fully immunised.
