There is no family history of haematological malignancy or bleeding disorders. She has no significant past medical history. Her appetite has decreased and she has lost about 1 kg in 4 weeks. She has no respiratory symptoms, no diarrhoea, and no rashes apart from the bruising. The GP has performed a full blood count today.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 130 bpm, RR 26 /min, SpO2 99%, BP 95/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pallor, scattered bruises over trunk and shins, petechiae on hard palate, hepatosplenomegaly with spleen tip 3 cm below the costal margin, bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy (largest 2 cm), no testicular mass, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Hb 68 g/L, WCC 28.4 x10^9/L (60% blasts on film), neutrophils 0.4 x10^9/L, platelets 18 x10^9/L, LDH 920 U/L, urate 0.45 mmol/L, UEC normal, LFTs mildly elevated, coagulation slightly deranged, blood film: blast cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio
data_table.History: 3 weeks pallor and bone pain, weight loss 1 kg, no recent viral illness, no antibiotics

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence empirical oral iron supplementation in the community and review the full blood count in 4 weeks today
options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts is not iron deficiency; iron is not appropriate and delays definitive diagnosis per RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer.

options.text: Transfuse packed red blood cells and platelets in the GP rooms today before referral to a tertiary paediatric centre
options.rationale: Transfusion in suspected leukaemia must occur in a paediatric oncology centre to avoid hyperviscosity and tumour lysis precipitation; pre-referral transfusion is inappropriate per RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer.

options.text: Refer urgently same-day to paediatric oncology for bone marrow aspirate, tumour lysis prevention, supportive care
options.rationale: Suspected acute leukaemia (anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, blasts on film, organomegaly) requires same-day transfer to a paediatric oncology centre for marrow diagnostics and management of tumour lysis risk per RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer and Cancer Council Australia: Paediatric oncology referral.
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: Paediatric oncology referral

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone pending paediatric oncology review at the tertiary centre this week as bridging therapy
options.rationale: Empirical corticosteroids before diagnosis can transiently lyse blasts and confound morphology and immunophenotyping; they are not given pre-marrow per RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer.

options.text: Arrange routine outpatient haematology review within 2 weeks and continue conservative observation with weekly bloods at the GP
options.rationale: A 2-week wait is unsafe with severe thrombocytopenia and neutropenia; same-day transfer is required per RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer.

explanation.summary: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is the commonest paediatric malignancy in Australia, with a peak incidence at age 2-5 and overall 5-year survival approaching 90% on modern protocols. Classical presentation combines pancytopenia symptoms (pallor, fatigue, bruising or bleeding, recurrent or severe infection), constitutional symptoms (low-grade fever, weight loss, anorexia), and infiltrative features (bone pain that wakes the child at night, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, mediastinal mass with T-cell disease). Diagnosis is by bone marrow aspirate showing over 20% lymphoblasts with immunophenotyping and cytogenetics. Pre-treatment priorities are tumour lysis syndrome prophylaxis (hydration at 2-3 L/m^2/day, allopurinol or rasburicase for high-risk), cardiac echo before anthracyclines, transfusion only in the paediatric centre (where leukoreduced irradiated products are available), and CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal methotrexate. Any febrile episode is treated as febrile neutropenia with same-day broad-spectrum IV antibiotics.
key_points: Triad to recognise: pancytopenia, organomegaly, blasts on film
Avoid steroids and avoid pre-referral transfusion
Tumour lysis precautions: hyperhydration, urate-lowering therapy
Definitive diagnosis: bone marrow aspirate and trephine with flow cytometry and cytogenetics
explanation.pearls: Bone pain that wakes a young child at night, pallor with bruising, and hepatosplenomegaly are the classic triad of paediatric ALL - urgent same-day referral to a tertiary paediatric oncology centre for bone marrow aspirate, tumour lysis prophylaxis (hydration plus allopurinol or rasburicase) and any transfusion (RCH CPG ALL).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: Paediatric oncology referral

stem:
A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) is brought to ED 20 minutes after eating a peanut-containing snack at a birthday party. He developed an urticarial rash, then lip and tongue swelling, then a hoarse voice and audible wheeze. His mother gave one dose of cetirizine and called an ambulance. He has a previous diagnosis of peanut allergy and carries an EpiPen Junior 150 microg which was not administered because his mother was not sure if this was severe enough.

He is fully immunised, has known mild asthma on no current preventer, and no other allergies. He has had no recent antibiotics. On paramedic arrival he had a single dose of IM adrenaline 150 microg into the lateral thigh, with partial improvement, then a second IM adrenaline dose at 5 minutes en route.

data_table.Vital signs: On ED arrival: HR 150 bpm, RR 34 /min, SpO2 93% on 6 L/min via Hudson mask, BP 88/52 mmHg, T 36.8 deg C
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, mild stridor, bilateral wheeze, capillary refill 3 seconds, GCS 15, no incontinence
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.36, lactate 2.4 mmol/L, glucose 5.6 mmol/L; tryptase taken; no other immediate labs
data_table.Medications: Cetirizine 5 mg PO 25 min ago, two doses IM adrenaline 150 microg, oxygen via mask
data_table.History: Known peanut allergy with EpiPen Jr, mild asthma off preventer

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg as primary treatment now and observe for 30 minutes before any further treatment escalation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are adjunctive only in anaphylaxis and have no role as primary therapy; IM adrenaline is first-line per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Repeat IM adrenaline 220 microg, give 440 mL saline bolus, then start adrenaline infusion if no response
options.rationale: Persistent anaphylaxis after two IM adrenaline doses warrants a third IM dose and an IV crystalloid bolus, with escalation to an IV adrenaline infusion if response remains inadequate per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Switch to IV adrenaline bolus 220 microg as initial escalation and repeat every 5 minutes as needed for ongoing shock
options.rationale: IV adrenaline boluses risk arrhythmia and myocardial ischaemia; infusion (not bolus) is the escalation route after IM doses per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as primary treatment for the bronchospasm and hold further IM adrenaline pending response
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is adjunctive for bronchospasm in anaphylaxis but does not replace IM adrenaline, which addresses upper airway oedema, bronchospasm and hypotension per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Give an additional dose of oral cetirizine 10 mg, observe for 30 minutes and reassess vital signs before any escalation
options.rationale: Antihistamines do not treat the airway or circulatory features of anaphylaxis and are adjunctive only per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with prompt IM adrenaline 10 microg/kg (max 500 microg) into the lateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Failure to respond to two IM doses warrants a third IM dose and an IV crystalloid bolus, then an IV adrenaline infusion. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjunctive only.
key_points: First-line: IM adrenaline 10 microg/kg lateral thigh
Refractory anaphylaxis: repeat IM adrenaline, IV fluid bolus, then IV adrenaline infusion
Adjuncts: oxygen, salbutamol for bronchospasm, antihistamines for rash
Observe at least 4 hours after stabilisation (longer if biphasic risk)
Discharge with EpiPen prescription, ASCIA Action Plan, and allergist referral
explanation.pearls: Beta-blocker therapy can blunt the adrenaline response; consider IV glucagon 20-30 microg/kg in refractory cases on a beta-blocker.

stem:
A 3-year-old previously well boy is brought to the GP by his mother with a 6-month history of infrequent painful bowel motions, on average every 4-5 days, with hard pellet-like or wide stools that sometimes block the toilet. There has been occasional faecal soiling between motions for the past 2 months. He passed meconium at 18 hours after birth and had no neonatal feeding concerns.

He is fully toilet-trained for urine and was day-trained for stool by age 2.5 but recently has been withholding. His diet is varied but low in fibre and high in cow's milk (around 700 mL per day). There is no abdominal distension when well, no vomiting, weight tracking along the 50th centile, and no family history of Hirschsprung disease. He has no perianal lesions and no neurological signs.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 100 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 100/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, palpable faecal mass in the left iliac fossa, no peritonism, normal anal position, no anal fissure, normal anal tone, normal lower limb tone and reflexes, growth on 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Bedside abdominal examination only; UEC and TFTs not indicated by history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric surgery for full-thickness rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease before any laxative therapy is commenced
options.rationale: Hirschsprung is unlikely given normal meconium passage, no failure to thrive, normal exam, and onset in toddler age with withholding; functional constipation is far more likely per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Disimpact with oral macrogol 3350 over 3-7 days then maintenance macrogol with toileting routine, dietary advice
options.rationale: Functional constipation with a palpable faecal mass is managed with oral macrogol disimpaction followed by maintenance, plus behavioural toileting routine and dietary modification per RCH CPG: Constipation and NICE NG99.

options.text: Commence a course of weekly phosphate enemas for the next 2 months as the primary disimpaction and maintenance strategy
options.rationale: Oral macrogol 3350 is the first-line disimpaction and maintenance agent in Australian and NICE guidance; enemas are reserved for failure of oral therapy or where oral access is impractical, and are not used as maintenance per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Restrict all dairy products from the diet immediately for 6 weeks and review the bowel habit and growth trajectory then
options.rationale: Excess cow's milk is a contributing factor but full restriction is not first-line; moderation plus disimpaction and maintenance laxatives are recommended per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Reassure the parents that bowel actions every 4-5 days are within the normal range and review in 6 months in clinic
options.rationale: Soiling, painful defecation and a palpable faecal mass meet criteria for functional constipation and require active treatment, not reassurance, per RCH CPG: Constipation.

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in toddlers is managed with oral macrogol disimpaction over several days followed by maintenance laxative therapy, toileting routine (sit-after-meals, footrest), and dietary advice (fibre, water, moderate dairy). Investigations are reserved for red flags suggesting Hirschsprung disease or organic causes.
key_points: Red flags for Hirschsprung: delayed meconium > 48 h, failure to thrive, ribbon stools from birth, palpable empty rectum on PR
Macrogol is first-line for disimpaction and maintenance
Enemas are second-line when oral fails
Treat for 6-12 months and wean slowly to prevent relapse
explanation.pearls: Reflex anal-cutaneous response (anal wink) and lower-limb neurology screen exclude tethered cord; document them.

stem:
A 14-month-old girl is brought to the GP because her mother is concerned she is not gaining weight. She was born at 38 weeks weighing 3.0 kg (25th centile). At 6 months she weighed 7.2 kg (25th centile). She now weighs 8.0 kg, which has fallen across two major weight centile lines and is now below the 3rd centile. Her length and head circumference remain on the 25th centile.

She is exclusively on family foods plus 600 mL of cow's milk per day; her mother reports prolonged mealtimes with frequent refusal of solids. She has no vomiting, no diarrhoea, and no fevers. She is fully immunised. The family lives in stable housing with no documented family violence. Developmentally she walks holding furniture, says two words, and points. There is no family history of coeliac disease or cystic fibrosis. She passed her newborn screen.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 115 bpm, RR 26 /min, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Alert and engaged, weight < 3rd centile having crossed 2 major centile lines, length on 25th centile, head circumference 25th centile, no dysmorphic features, mildly reduced subcutaneous fat over buttocks, abdomen soft, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Office observation; no bloods done yet; growth chart available since birth
data_table.History: Cow's milk 600 mL/day, prolonged mealtimes, food refusal, no GI symptoms, normal development apart from feeding

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for inpatient nasogastric feeding, baseline screening blood panel, dietitian review, and consideration of safeguarding referral as inpatient
options.rationale: Inpatient NG feeding is reserved for severe faltering growth with safeguarding concerns, failure of community management, or severe malnutrition; outpatient assessment is first-line here per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

options.text: Detailed feeding history, growth chart review, baseline bloods and urinalysis, reduce cow's milk under 500 mL/day, dietitian referral
options.rationale: Faltering growth crossing two centiles is investigated with a structured history, growth-chart review, a focused screening panel, dietary modification (limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day to prevent iron deficiency and energy displacement), and multidisciplinary outpatient review per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive and NICE NG75.

options.text: Reassure as normal toddler variation, continue current diet including 600 mL cow's milk per day, and review in 6 months in clinic
options.rationale: Falling across 2 major weight centiles is significant faltering growth requiring assessment, not delayed review, per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

options.text: Commence high-calorie infant formula supplements 200 mL three times daily for 6 weeks without further dietary or medical assessment today
options.rationale: Empirical formula supplementation without identifying cause and engaging dietitians is not best practice; the underlying contribution (excess cow's milk, behavioural feeding pattern) must be addressed per NICE NG75.

options.text: Refer immediately to state child protection services for suspected nutritional neglect before any further medical or dietetic assessment is done
options.rationale: Without specific safeguarding red flags, the first step is medical and dietetic assessment; child protection referral is reserved for cases with neglect features per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

explanation.summary: Faltering growth in toddlerhood is most commonly nutritional rather than organic, but a structured workup is mandatory before reassurance. A detailed feeding history captures volume of milk and solids, mealtime behaviour, parental feeding style, and any aspirations or fatigue. Plot serial weight, length and head circumference on the WHO growth charts: isolated weight faltering with preserved length and head circumference suggests under-nutrition; symmetrical faltering of all three points raises chronic disease or syndromic causes. First-line investigations are FBC, ferritin, UEC, LFTs, TFTs, coeliac serology (if eating gluten), urinalysis and stool microscopy. Excess cow's milk (above 500-600 mL/day) is a common displaceable contributor - it suppresses appetite and causes occult iron deficiency. Management is multidisciplinary: dietitian-led nutritional optimisation, paediatrician for organic workup, feeding therapist if behavioural component. Admission for NG feeding is reserved for severe failure or social safety concerns. Child protection escalation requires demonstrable neglect rather than isolated growth failure.
key_points: Define faltering growth by crossing centile lines, not by absolute weight alone
Limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day in toddlers
Baseline workup: FBC, UEC, LFTs, iron studies, coeliac serology, TFTs, urinalysis
Multidisciplinary outpatient management first; admit if severe or unsafe
Consider coeliac, cow's milk protein allergy, CF, malabsorption, behavioural factors
explanation.pearls: Excess cow's milk above 500-600 mL/day displaces solids and causes occult iron deficiency in Australian toddlers; reducing milk, optimising solids, and a screening panel (FBC, ferritin, UEC, LFT, urinalysis, coeliac serology if appropriate) precede any inpatient or child protection escalation (RCH CPG Faltering Growth).
